apex: Most personal OKRs get abandoned by week 3.
verdict: Verdict: Event-based habit cues show 66% week 3 retention vs 36% for standard outcome OKRs; tool reminders (38%) do not resolve the habit constraint.

nodes.text: Cohort A (standard outcome goals) drops to 36% week 3 retention (n=50, Mar-Apr), whereas Cohort B (habit cue goals) maintains 66% retention (n=50).
Cohort C (tool reminders) achieves only 38% week 3 retention (n=50), indicating that push notifications do not bypass the habit constraint.
Writing explicit trigger-action cues (82% in Cohort B vs 10% in Cohort A) strongly correlates with increased OKR retention.

roots.title: Setting OKRs as outcomes without establishing habit triggers or calendar review slots (POLICY constraint)
roots.action: Require users to write event-based trigger-action cues and schedule recurring calendar review slots.

children.text: Users fail to track goals because they lack a concrete mental association between a routine event and the tracking action.

constraint.statement: Lack of event-based triggers and scheduled reviews in goal design.
constraint.ifFalse: If cognitive triggers are not the constraint, the constraint shifts to social accountability (Cohort D).

negatives: Deploying automated push notifications (Cohort C), which fail to establish internal tracking habits and increase tool fatigue.

tests.test: Conduct an A/B test with three groups: Group 1 (Habit cues only), Group 2 (Review slots only), Group 3 (Both).
tests.decides: Isolates the independent impact of habit-cue triggers vs scheduled calendar reviews on retention.

census.bottomLine: This is a data-grounded tree. 50% of the nodes are MEASURED from cohort outcomes.
